claim.content: Average task success rates across the evaluated models increase monotonically as primitive abstraction increases from Low-Level tiers (S3/S4, where agents compose atomic perception and control primitives) to High-Level tiers (S1/S2, where agents call human-crafted macro primitives), indicating that providing human-designed high-level primitives that collapse perception, geometry, and control complexity substantially improves zero-shot programmatic control performance relative to operating directly over atomic interfaces.

conclusion.content: Average task success rates across the evaluated models increase monotonically as primitive abstraction increases from Low-Level tiers (S3/S4, where agents compose atomic perception and control primitives) to High-Level tiers (S1/S2, where agents call human-crafted macro primitives), indicating that providing human-designed high-level primitives that collapse perception, geometry, and control complexity substantially improves zero-shot programmatic control performance relative to operating directly over atomic interfaces.

premises.content:
The reported benchmark aggregates show a monotonic increase in average task success rate as primitive abstraction increases from Low-Level tiers (S3/S4) to High-Level tiers (S1/S2), and the paper attributes these gains to human-crafted high-level primitives that collapse perception, geometric reasoning, and low-level control into coarser macros that reduce the effective search space for program synthesis.
      Fig. 3

steps.reasoning: Using CaP-Bench’s controlled abstraction axis (high-level primitives in S1/S2 versus low-level primitives in S3/S4), the paper aggregates performance across models to study how task success varies with the primitive abstraction level.

The paper reports a qualitative trend: “Figure 3 shows a monotonic increase in task success as primitive abstraction increases,” which directly asserts that success rates rise as the benchmark moves from low-level tiers toward high-level tiers.

The paper also verbalizes the direction of this increase: “As primitive abstraction increases S4 … to S1 … the success rate increases,” explicitly connecting lower abstraction tiers (S3/S4) to lower success and higher abstraction tiers (S1/S2) to higher success.

The interpretation provided is that human-designed primitives “collapse low-level perception, geometric reasoning, and control into human-designed primitives,” thereby “reduc[ing] the effective search space and allow[ing] models to focus on task sequencing,” which is used as the mechanism explaining why higher-level abstractions boost success.

The paper frames this as dependence on engineered abstractions: the success improvement is attributed to “human-crafted abstractions,” and correspondingly “performance degradation at lower abstraction levels (S3/S4) reflects the difficulty of code synthesis,” reinforcing that the same tasks become harder when the engineered abstraction is removed.

The empirical support is the figure described in the text as “Average task success rate across open-source and closed-source models as primitive abstraction increases,” which (as described) shows success rising when moving from S4 to S1. Fig. 3

motivating_questions.content: Prior Code-as-Policy robotics systems often report strong performance while relying on human-designed high-level primitives that compress complex subroutines (perception, geometric reasoning, control) into single calls. This creates a confound: observed task success may reflect the inductive bias and reduced search space imposed by the engineered API rather than the agent's ability to synthesize correct low-level code and recovery logic. A systematic characterization of how performance changes as these priors are removed is necessary to understand the true capabilities and limitations of coding agents and to avoid overestimating generality based on over-engineered abstractions.

paper:1243508795896758359.en_title: CaP-X: A Framework for Benchmarking and Improving Coding Agents for Robot Manipulation
paper:1243508795896758359.zh_title: CaP-X：一个用于对机器人操作的编码智能体进行基准测试和改进的框架
